mappings.rationale: Directly covers quantum mechanics including the wave-mechanical theory of electronic structure, quantized transitions, and the Bohr model central to atomic structure.
Addresses atomic structure, electron configurations, and periodic properties arising from the arrangement and behaviour of electrons described in this section.
Covers the nature and properties of atoms including atomic weights, isotopic composition, and the historical discovery of atomic structure.
Covers the nucleus surrounded by electrons, nuclear models including the Rutherford model, and the relationship between nuclear and atomic structure.
Directly relevant to the section's coverage of antimatter, antiparticles, charge conjugation invariance, and fundamental physical constants.
Explores concepts of the vacuum, antimatter, and the nature of fundamental particles and constants relevant to this section's discussion of antimatter.
Covers atomic structure, electron configurations, and the developments in chemistry that provided scientific evidence for the existence and nature of atoms.
Covers the historical development of atomic models from Rutherford to Bohr-Sommerfeld to wave mechanics, and the discoveries of X rays, electrons, and radioactivity.
Directly relevant to the Bohr-Sommerfeld model of the atom and the classical Bohr theory of atomic spectra that are central topics in this section.
Covers the atomic nature of matter, atomic structure, and the fundamental properties of atoms and their interactions.
Relevant to atomic spectra, emission and absorption of light by atoms, and the development of spectroscopy discussed throughout this section.
Covers atomic interactions and how the arrangement of electrons determines chemical bonding, connecting to the section's focus on atomic interactions.
Directly relevant to the section's coverage of the discovery of radioactive transformation of elements as evidence for atomic structure.
Relevant to X-ray diffraction by crystals, a key application of X rays discussed in this section for probing atomic structure.
Directly relevant to the section's coverage of isotopic composition, variations in atomic weight, and mass spectrometry methods.
Covers atomic spectra, spectroscopy, quantum mechanical treatments of atomic structure, and the wave-mechanical theory central to this section.
Relevant to the effects of magnetic fields on atomic spectra and magnetic resonance phenomena discussed in the context of atomic structure.
Provides broad coverage of atomic physics including atomic structure, spectra, and the fundamental constants discussed in this section.
Covers electron behaviour in matter and quantum mechanical treatments relevant to understanding atomic properties and electronic structure.
Covers foundational concepts in quantum mechanics and wave-particle duality central to the wave-mechanical theory of atomic structure.
Relevant to the historical development of spectroscopy and the discovery of electromagnetic phenomena underlying atomic spectra.
Provides context for early philosophical speculations on atomism and the development of scientific evidence for the atomic nature of matter.
Relevant to the measurement of fundamental physical constants and precision methods for determining atomic weights discussed in this section.
Covers atomic-scale imaging and manipulation techniques including scanning probe microscopy that reveal atomic structure directly.
Relevant to electron microscopy and imaging techniques used to visualize atomic and subatomic structures discussed in this section.
